ExplorationService interface declares 'throws IOException' on all four methods, but SemanticFieldExplorer (the sole implementation) declares 'throws ExplorationException' and never propagates IOException — instead wrapping it. ExplorationException extends RuntimeException, so it is unchecked.

In HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling, ExplorationException bypasses catch(IOException e) and falls into catch(Exception e), logged as 'unexpected error' — masking a known, expected failure mode (corpus query failed) as an uncharacterised runtime surprise.
ExplorationHandlers methods declare 'throws IOException' because of the interface contract, but IOException can never actually propagate from them — the contract is vacuously declared.
BlackLabQueryExecutor constructor explicitly catches ErrorOpeningIndex (RuntimeException) and converts it to a checked IOException with preserved context — correct boundary translation.
CollocateQueryHelper consistently converts InvalidQuery to IllegalArgumentException (mapped to 400), and wraps RuntimeException as IOException for unexpected BlackLab failures — appropriate layering.
GrammarConfigLoader uses a documented two-tier strategy: checked IOException for callers that handle it, IllegalStateException wrapping for startup paths — intentional and well-explained.
All HTTP handlers route through wrapWithErrorHandling with a consistent mapping: IllegalArgumentException -> 400, IOException -> 500, Exception -> 500.
Parameter validation (HttpApiUtils.requireParam, parseIntParam, parseDoubleParam) throws IllegalArgumentException consistently across all handlers.
No silent error swallowing observed — every catch either rethrows, wraps, or sends an error response.
Domain vocabulary (seed, collocate, lemma, logDice, relation, BCQL/CQL) is used uniformly across all layers — query, exploration, model, and API.

`buildBcqlWithLemmaSubstitution` (BlackLabQueryExecutor.java) says 'substitution' but the implementation simply prepends a quoted lemma token before the collocate constraint: `String.format("\"%s\" %s", ...)`. The Javadoc itself uses the word 'prepend', not 'substitute'.

`executeCollocateSearchWithContent` vs `executeCollocateSearch` (CollocateQueryHelper.java): the 'WithContent' suffix is vague — 'content' resolves only in the Javadoc to mean 'stored XML fields'. The underlying boolean flag is `withStoredHits`, which is more self-documenting.

high_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase sits comfortably at the 90-anchor boundary: ownership is clear, package boundaries are documented and respected by all active code, and the decomposition is coherent enough that a new engineer could explain every package from the package-info alone. The one concrete defect — a dead api/viz/RadialPlot.java copy that directly violates the rule stated in viz/package-info.java — prevents a 90 score but does not rise to the 80-anchor pattern of recurring friction, since no production code actually uses the stale copy. A score of 84 reflects strong structural clarity with one documented-but-violated boundary.

convention_outlier.issue_character: The issues are isolated structural outliers — a dead duplicate class and a shadow package — rather than pervasive naming drift; the main body of the codebase is coherent, but the two outliers create genuine navigational confusion about which copy is canonical.
convention_outlier.score_rationale: The codebase earns a solid baseline: naming is uniform, package responsibilities are documented, and the handler/assembler/model layers are clearly delineated. Two convention outliers pull the score down: api/viz/RadialPlot.java is a near-identical dead copy that contradicts the viz package's own boundary documentation, and model/sketch/ is a shadow package of QueryResults nested types that was never activated, leaving four unreferenced record types that imply an abandoned migration. These are real developer confusion points — a contributor encountering two RadialPlot classes in different packages, or four model/sketch types that appear to be the 'flat' canonical forms but are never used, must investigate before trusting the code. At a global 80=solid-but-uneven anchor, these two localized structural inconsistencies bring the score to approximately 70.

strengths: wrapWithErrorHandling provides a single, centralized HTTP error boundary with consistent status code mapping across all endpoints, eliminating per-handler boilerplate error logic.
BlackLabQueryExecutor constructor correctly translates BlackLab's RuntimeException (ErrorOpeningIndex) into a checked IOException, giving callers a predictable contract at the index-open boundary.
CollocateQueryHelper consistently converts the library-specific InvalidQuery into IllegalArgumentException (→ 400) at every call site — no leakage of library exception types to HTTP consumers.
No error swallowing found: every catch block either rethrows, wraps with context, or sends a structured error response.
GrammarConfigLoader's two-tier exception strategy (checked for I/O paths, IllegalStateException for startup paths) is deliberate and documented.
error_consistency.issue_character: A single structural inconsistency at the ExplorationService boundary: the interface declares checked IOException but the implementation throws unchecked ExplorationException, causing exploration failures to be logged as 'unexpected errors' rather than expected I/O failures — isolated but affecting the predictability of one entire subsystem.
error_consistency.score_rationale: The codebase has a coherent, centrally-enforced error strategy: one error boundary wrapper, consistent exception-to-status-code mapping, no silent swallowing, and appropriate checked/unchecked distinctions at most boundaries. The one real gap is the ExplorationService interface/implementation contract mismatch — a developer working with the interface would expect IOException but get ExplorationException (unchecked), and exploration failures appear as 'unexpected errors' in logs rather than the expected 'description failed' path. This pulls the score below the 80–90 range for 'solid but uneven': the codebase is largely coherent but a developer relying on the interface contract would be surprised in production.

strengths: Domain vocabulary is consistent and precise codebase-wide: seed, collocate, lemma, logDice, BCQL/CQL, relation — a developer can read names in one layer and immediately recognise them in another.

Service and executor names communicate contracts precisely: `SemanticFieldExplorer`, `CollocateProfileComparator`, `BlackLabQueryExecutor`, `CollocateQueryHelper` — no generic Manager/Processor/Util clusters.
Model types are domain-appropriate and specific: `DiscoveredNoun`, `CoreCollocate`, `CollocateProfile`, `ExplorationResult` describe their roles without ambiguity.

HTTP handler method names follow `handleXxx` consistently across all handler classes, and the grouped-handler decomposition (ConcordanceHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, SketchHandlers) accurately names what each group owns.
Enum and constant names communicate intent: `RelationEdgeType.SEED_ADJ`, `DISCOVERED_ADJ`, `MODIFIER`; `CROSS_RELATIONAL` sentinel is clearly documented.
naming_quality.issue_character: Issues are isolated naming imprecisions — a method name that implies substitution where there is none, a vague suffix that requires Javadoc to interpret, and a field-name collision in one internal class — rather than a systemic vocabulary problem or codebase-wide generic-verb drift.
naming_quality.score_rationale: The codebase sits comfortably above the 80-anchor (recurring friction) because the vast majority of names precisely communicate intent and the domain vocabulary is coherent throughout. It falls below the 90-anchor (trust what you see, minor friction only) because two method names (`buildBcqlWithLemmaSubstitution`, `executeCollocateSearchWithContent`) require callers to consult Javadoc to understand the actual behaviour, and the `radius`/`circleRadius` pair in RadialPlot creates momentary geometric confusion. These are isolated issues, not a pattern, which places the score at approximately 83.

strengths: ExplorationService and QueryExecutor single-implementation interfaces are documented with explicit design rationale — the Javadoc on ExplorationService explains exactly why the interface exists despite having one implementation (testability, unified feature surface).

Interface segregation is applied in practice: CorpusQueryHandlers and ConcordanceHandlers take the narrower CollocateQueryPort rather than the full QueryExecutor, demonstrating the interfaces are actually leveraged.
HttpApiUtils achieves genuine cohesion — every method serves HTTP I/O concerns (param parsing, response writing, error mapping, CORS) with no cross-domain leakage.
ExploreResponseAssembler earns its layer by owning the entire model-to-presentation translation, keeping ExplorationResult and ComparisonResult free of JSON concerns.
RelationPatternUtils is a justified extraction from RelationConfig, keeping the config record as a plain data carrier while centralising stateless pattern-building logic.
abstraction_fitness.issue_character: Issues are localized to one incomplete migration: the model.sketch.* package was created to promote QueryResults nested types to standalone top-level records but was never completed, leaving identical duplicate types that are dead code.
abstraction_fitness.score_rationale: The abstractions in this codebase mostly pay for themselves: interfaces are documented with clear rationale, utility classes are cohesive, and the exploration layer decomposes cleanly into focused components. The one concrete structural problem — an abortive migration leaving four unused duplicate model types in model.sketch.* — prevents a higher score by introducing a confusing shadow type hierarchy. This single-source issue is localized to the model package, does not affect runtime behavior, and is straightforward to resolve, placing the codebase solidly at the 80 anchor (solid but with a recurring friction point a developer would notice when navigating model types).

strengths: Single JSON library: Jackson is used consistently everywhere; no competing JSON libs (no Fastjson, Gson, or org.json present)
Canonical SLF4J + Logback logging pattern: all source code logs via the SLF4J facade, with a single logback-classic runtime implementation, no duplicate logging frameworks
Both BlackLab modules are genuinely used: blacklab-engine powers indexing/querying, blacklab-query-parser provides the CQL parsers referenced directly in query execution code
jspecify correctly scoped as 'provided' — annotation-only library carries zero runtime weight
log4j-to-slf4j bridge is purposeful: it routes BlackLab's internal Log4J output through SLF4J rather than introducing a second logging sink
dependency_health.issue_character: Issues are minor and isolated: one dead repository declaration (UFAL) with no corresponding dependency, and a slightly redundant jackson-core explicit pin that is already managed by the imported BOM.
dependency_health.score_rationale: The dependency set is lean, purposeful, and free of the classic pathologies — no duplicate libraries for the same purpose, no heavy deps for trivial use, no unused production dependencies. The codebase aligns closely with the 90-anchor ('mostly healthy with minor overlap or weight concerns'). The UFAL dead repository entry and the verbose jackson-core pin are the only rough edges, neither of which causes runtime problems or creates real developer friction, keeping the score just below 90.

strengths: Phase-based decomposition is applied consistently across algorithm classes: SingleSeedExplorer, MultiSeedExplorer, and CollocateProfileComparator all break complex algorithms into clearly-named private methods with bounded responsibilities.

Private records (HitRecord, CollocateSearch, GroupStats, SharedExploreParams, SeedCollocateData, AggregatedCollocateStats, RadialRequest) are used systematically to group related data without creating heavyweight types, keeping method signatures concise.
State mutation is tightly local throughout: mutable accumulators (CollocateAccumulator, frequency maps) are created, mutated, and consumed within a single method and never escape their scope.
BlackLabSnippetParser's regex patterns are pre-compiled as static final constants and method boundaries are precise — each method does exactly one extraction task with no cross-cutting concerns.
HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling maps three distinct exception types to distinct HTTP status codes with no ambiguity; the branches are necessary and non-overlapping.
low_level_elegance.issue_character: Issues are scattered minor inconsistencies rather than systematic problems: a null sentinel where an enum value is available, an unexplained magic formula, one FQN inconsistency, a duplicated guard, and a test-seam nullable field that requires internal assertion wrappers instead of parameter injection.
low_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase sits solidly above the 'significant drag' threshold (60) and approaches 'solid but uneven' (80). The algorithm decomposition and bounded-state practices are exemplary for this domain. What pulls the score below 80 is a cluster of small but real friction points: the CollocateQueryHelper nullable-index test seam forces every I/O method to call requireIndex() as a defensive assertion rather than receiving a guaranteed non-null argument; the resolvePosGroupFromPrefix null return propagates an unnecessary nullable contract to its caller; and the magic 1/3 core-collocate threshold leaves an important tuning decision undocumented. None of these are deep structural problems, but together they represent a modest but real gap from exemplary low-level clarity.

strengths: ExploreResponseAssembler is a dedicated translation layer: all model-to-response conversion for exploration endpoints lives in one place, keeping ExplorationResult/ComparisonResult free of presentation concerns.
ExplorationService interface provides a clean, minimal API→service seam; ExplorationHandlers only sees the interface, not the concrete implementation.
GrammarConfigSerializer cleanly separates JSON serialisation from value objects, so GrammarConfig and RelationConfig carry no JSON library dependency.
GrammarConfigLoader documents its two-tier exception strategy (IOException vs IllegalArgumentException) explicitly, making error propagation across the load boundary unsurprising.
Handler decomposition (ConcordanceHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, SketchHandlers) distributes HTTP concerns cleanly without cross-handler coupling.
mid_level_elegance.issue_character: Inconsistent assembly conventions within the response-building layer — mix of map-mutation and typed-return patterns — create implicit contracts and duplication that a developer extending the API must navigate without a clear model to follow.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: The higher-level seams (ExplorationService interface, GrammarConfigSerializer, ExploreResponseAssembler as a single translation home) are genuinely well-designed and score toward the 80-90 range on their own. What pulls the score down is the inconsistency within ExploreResponseAssembler itself: it mixes mutating-map handoffs (populateExploreResponse, populateComparisonResponse) with return-typed-value handoffs (buildExamplesResponse), and the BaseEnvelope pattern exposes a live mutable sub-map for post-construction mutation that leaks construction intent across the boundary. These create recurring 'which pattern applies here?' friction for developers extending or testing the API layer, placing this solidly at 73 — solid but uneven, matching the '80: developer hits recurring friction' anchor pulled slightly lower by the concrete duplication and mutable-ref leak.

strengths: Docstrings across GrammarConfigLoader, RelationConfig, ExplorationHandlers, and SemanticFieldExplorer consistently explain non-obvious design decisions (Optional-as-field antipattern rationale, exception-type contract, handler asymmetry note, pass-through-facade justification) rather than restating what the code already says.
Logging is deliberate and targeted: debug-level for algorithm tracing, warn-level for tolerable per-relation failures, no function-entry/exit noise anywhere in the codebase.

Domain-specific naming used uniformly throughout — exploreByRelation, compareCollocateProfiles, resolveRelationAlias, fetchSeedCollocates, buildCollocateReversePattern — no generic handleData or processItem patterns found.
Exception handling is intentional rather than boilerplate: ExplorationException wraps IOException at a meaningful service boundary with context-rich messages, and the rationale is documented inline.
No defensive null checks on typed non-nullable values; @NonNull/@Nullable annotations are used to express intent rather than adding redundant runtime guards.
ai_generated_debt.issue_character: A single isolated dead pass-through wrapper (SketchHandlers.handleDependencyRelationQuery) is the main AI-hallmark defect; minor docstring-restatement tendencies in serializer and CLI-param helpers are present but brief and non-pervasive.
ai_generated_debt.score_rationale: The codebase sits well above average on this dimension: comments explain design rationale, logging is purposeful, and naming is domain-specific throughout. The one clear defect — a dead private method that only delegates to another method with a hardcoded enum — is localised to a single file and is a minor structural noise. A few terse restating summaries in serializer and CLI helpers are borderline but not verbose enough to drag the score significantly. This places the codebase in the 80-85 range: solid practitioner-authored quality with one small isolated AI-hallmark pattern.

strengths: GrammarConfigLoader actively fails fast on old config keys ('cql_pattern', 'copulas') with actionable error messages, ensuring the grammar-config migration is enforced, not merely documented
No dual-library coexistence: a single JSON library (Jackson) is used uniformly; no traces of fastjson, Gson, or other alternatives
No deprecated-but-called symbols found; the codebase has no @Deprecated annotations on live call paths
The HTTP server choice (com.sun.net.httpserver) is documented as an intentional deliberate decision, not an abandoned migration — the TODO is explicitly conditional on a future requirement
incomplete_migration.issue_character: The only migration residue is two isolated documentation artifacts — a stale Javadoc package reference and a historical 'moved from' comment — rather than any live code coexistence or abandoned migration paths.
incomplete_migration.score_rationale: This codebase scores well on the incomplete_migration dimension because migrations appear to have been completed and enforced: old config keys are actively rejected, no dual API patterns coexist, and no deprecated symbols are called. The score is held below 90 by two minor documentation-level residues — a stale Javadoc cross-reference pointing to the wrong package for RelationPatternUtils, and a historical comment in CqlUtils recording where code was moved from. These are local, non-confusing artifacts rather than structural migration debt, placing the codebase at the high end of the 'solid but uneven' range rather than 'exemplary'.

strengths: Package names are unambiguous and match responsibilities: api, config, exploration, indexer, model, query, utils, viz each contain only files consistent with the package's declared purpose.
The model/exploration vs exploration split correctly separates pure DTOs from service logic, a textbook example of the kind of boundary that prevents coupling drift.
Every package has a package-info.java with a one-sentence scope statement, which aids navigation significantly.
The api/ package correctly uses package-private visibility for response DTOs (ExamplesResponse) rather than leaking them into model, keeping the HTTP contract scoped to the handler layer.
package_organization.issue_character: Two dead-code structural artifacts — an unreferenced model/sketch/ sub-package and a duplicate api/viz/RadialPlot.java — create false navigability targets that do not participate in any active import graph, representing stalled migration fragments rather than systemic placement errors.
package_organization.score_rationale: The codebase demonstrates deliberate, well-documented package design: clear single-responsibility packages, correct DTO/service separation, package-info coverage, and consistent naming. What pulls the score below 80 is the presence of two dead structural artifacts (model/sketch/ with four unreferenced duplicate types, and api/viz/RadialPlot.java with zero importers) that a developer discovering the codebase for the first time would reasonably treat as live API surface. These are localized placement problems, not systemic boundary failures, so the score stays well above 60.

strengths: GrammarConfigLoader's two-tier exception contract (IOException for I/O failures, IllegalArgumentException for structural/validation failures) is the best example in the codebase: it is documented at the class level and consistently honored across all entry points.

HttpApiUtils parameter helpers form a coherent, uniform contract layer — every helper follows the same pattern of throwing IllegalArgumentException for client errors, making the HTTP tier predictable.

QueryExecutor precisely separates executeCqlQuery from executeBcqlQuery and executeSurfacePattern with precise Javadoc explaining the different parsers, labeling semantics, and scoring behaviours — these are contracts that could easily be blurred but aren't.
CollocateQueryPort as a narrower interface for handlers needing only BCQL retrieval is an honest division: handlers that accept CollocateQueryPort get exactly what they use.

CollocateQueryHelper documents the test-seam constructor explicitly, warning that the no-index constructor is not for production use and that requireIndex() will throw IllegalStateException — an honest contract for a deliberate design seam.
contract_coherence.issue_character: The main contract problems are interface/implementation divergence: ExplorationService lies about its checked-exception surface (declares IOException but implementation throws unchecked ExplorationException), and the model/sketch package presents a parallel type hierarchy as canonical when it is entirely dead code, contradicting the model package-info.
contract_coherence.score_rationale: The codebase demonstrates strong contract discipline in its low-level utility and configuration layers, where exceptions, parameter semantics, and nullability are documented and honored. However, the ExplorationService/ExplorationException mismatch is a subsystem-level interface lie: callers coding against the published throws-IOException contract will write dead error-handling code while real exceptions escape unchecked. The model/sketch parallel-dead-type problem further erodes type-discipline by presenting two contradicting narratives about the canonical location of core result types. These are not trivial: they would mislead the next developer writing a new ExplorationService caller or a new result handler. A codebase with otherwise good contract hygiene held back by two meaningful coherence failures lands in the high-60s to low-70s range.

strengths: Guard conditions before LogDiceUtils.compute() are applied consistently in both buildAndRankCollocates and scoreHits, preventing IllegalArgumentException from leaking
Early-exit guards for null/empty inputs (executeCollocations, executeDependencyPattern) are applied cleanly and uniformly across the query layer
The two-phase BCQL pipeline (collectHits → buildCollocateFrequencyMap → scoreHits) has no hidden control flow; each phase has a single clear responsibility
No identical if/else branches, always-true/false conditions, or boolean simplification opportunities were found in the reviewed files
logic_clarity.issue_character: The single identified issue is a structurally unreachable trace-log guard in a single method — isolated dead code that does not affect runtime behavior or mislead a developer reading surrounding logic.
logic_clarity.score_rationale: The codebase sits solidly in the 90-anchor range: control flow is direct, guard conditions are consistent, and a developer tracing the collocate pipeline or exploration service would encounter no surprises. One isolated dead branch (the jointFreq == 0 trace-log guard in scoreHits) prevents a 90+ score because it represents logic that cannot execute and whose message would be misleading if it ever did. At 88.0 the score reflects a strong overall quality with one minor, localized clarity gap.

strengths: jspecify @NonNull/@Nullable annotations are applied consistently across the entire public API surface: QueryExecutor, ExplorationService, HttpApiUtils, ExploreResponseAssembler, and BlackLabSnippetParser all annotate parameters and returns
Nullable returns are accurately modeled in BlackLabSnippetParser (extractLemmaFromMatch, extractPosFromMatch, safeParts return @Nullable and callers check before use)

Java records are used for all internal data-transfer bundles (GroupStats, AggregatedCollocateStats, SeedCollocateData, SharedExploreParams, BcqlRequest, RadialRequest), eliminating mutable-field type confusion
QueryExecutor interface Javadoc documents return-type semantics precisely (e.g. 'returns empty list silently if null or empty' for the @Nullable lemma parameter)
type_safety.issue_character: The most substantive issue is a single structural type-contract lie: the ExplorationService interface declares checked IOException on all methods while the sole implementation always converts IOException to an unchecked ExplorationException, meaning callers using the interface contract would write incorrect catch blocks; the remaining issues are isolated annotation omissions on config parameters in utility classes.
type_safety.score_rationale: The annotation discipline is strong for a Java codebase — jspecify coverage of public APIs is near-complete and the nullability modeling in BlackLabSnippetParser and QueryExecutor is accurate. However, the ExplorationService interface promising 'throws IOException' while the implementation never throws it as a checked exception is a genuine type-contract dishonesty that would mislead callers writing defensive code against the interface. The missing @NonNull on config parameters in GrammarConfigSerializer and RelationPatternUtils are minor gaps. Overall this sits in the solid-but-uneven range (high 70s): excellent annotation coverage pulls the score up, but the interface exception-type mismatch prevents it from reaching the 80+ range.

strengths: RadialPlot has a single coherent responsibility split cleanly across constructor (layout) and toSVG() (rendering)
All scaling constants are grouped and named by concern at the top of the class, making the design intent clear
The Item and Collocate inner classes are appropriately scoped and minimal — no unnecessary exposure
The renderFromItems static factory is a clean entry point that composes construction and rendering without leaking internals
design_coherence.issue_character: The single issue is a classic copy-paste artifact: an entire class was duplicated into a second package and then abandoned — it contributes nothing and creates silent maintenance risk.
design_coherence.score_rationale: The design of the RadialPlot class itself is solid: focused responsibility, clean constants, appropriate inner classes. The score is held back by the presence of a complete dead duplicate in api/viz — not merely similar but structurally identical, suggesting it was copied during a refactor and never cleaned up. This is a localized issue with no architectural consequence beyond the risk of future divergence, placing it in the 70-75 range rather than 80+.

strengths: Acyclic dependency graph with clear layer order: utils/model → config → query/exploration → api, enforced consistently across all packages
Interface segregation applied at two points: CollocateQueryPort (narrow slice for BCQL-only callers) and ExplorationService (full service for api), with QueryExecutor extending CollocateQueryPort
model and utils are clean leaf packages with zero internal imports, making them freely reusable without any blast-radius risk
Main.java is the only composition root that wires concrete types; all other modules depend on interfaces or DTOs
Package-info Javadoc documents boundary contracts and explains design decisions (why QueryResults stays in model root, why exploration subtypes live in model/exploration)
cross_module_architecture.issue_character: The issues are isolated and shallow: the interface/implementation boundary within the exploration package is blurred by colocation (no module-info.java), and one small utility class (Seeds) leaked into the exploration package rather than utils, creating a minor but unnecessary dependency from api to exploration concrete infrastructure.
cross_module_architecture.score_rationale: The codebase demonstrates strong architectural discipline — clean layer ordering, no cycles, good interface segregation, and a proper composition root. These are non-trivial to achieve and are consistently applied. The gap from exemplary (90+) is the absence of enforced module boundaries (no module-info.java), the cohabitation of ExplorationService interface and its implementations in a single package (api cannot be prevented from seeing concrete exploration classes), and the Seeds utility misplaced in exploration rather than utils. These are real but minor friction points that a developer would notice only when considering testability or enforcing strict boundaries — they do not impede daily work. Score: 80 (solid but with a clear path to tighter encapsulation).

strengths: System property reads (cors.allow.origin, grammar.config) are deferred to method-call time rather than class-load time, so tests can inject values without static coupling.
All wiring in Main.java is explicit and linearly sequenced — no hidden boot-order contracts between modules.
WordSketchApiServer separates construction from port binding, enabling side-effect-free instantiation in tests.
No static initializer blocks with I/O, network, or environment reads anywhere in the production source tree.
JsonUtils.MAPPER private-with-copy pattern prevents any consumer from mutating the shared instance.
initialization_coupling.issue_character: A single isolated weakness: HttpApiUtils.MAPPER is package-visible instead of private, making the mutable ObjectMapper reachable within the api package — an accidental configure() call would silently affect all per-request serialization.
initialization_coupling.score_rationale: The codebase demonstrates deliberate, disciplined attention to initialization coupling: all environment reads are deferred, all construction is explicit and side-effect-free, and no global mutable singletons exist with order dependencies. The one gap — a package-visible mutable ObjectMapper — is real but confined to a single package and requires an unlikely in-package accident to manifest. Against the 90-anchor ('mostly stable with limited boot-order fragility'), this codebase exceeds that bar with only a trivial residual risk, placing it just above 90.

strengths: Pervasive StubQueryExecutor pattern decouples every business-logic layer from the corpus index, enabling fast, deterministic unit tests for exploration, comparison, and ranking
No snapshot tests — all assertions check named properties, so tests remain stable through refactors
BlackLabIntegrationTest provides CI-safe coverage of the full internal query pipeline (parse → accumulate → score → rank) without requiring a live index
Live index tests are properly guarded with JUnit Assumptions so CI never fails due to missing infrastructure
WordSketchApiServerTest delivers genuine full-stack coverage by starting a real HTTP server and exercising request routing, param validation, error handling, and JSON serialisation end-to-end
test_strategy.issue_character: The test investment is concentrated in the upper business-logic layers (exploration, comparison, API handlers), while the lowest-level corpus-access path — the BlackLab executor and indexer — is structurally excluded from CI by missing external infrastructure, leaving the runtime critical path untested automatically.
test_strategy.score_rationale: The codebase demonstrates clear intent and real skill in test strategy: consistent stub patterns, no fragility, proper gating of live tests, and end-to-end HTTP tests. This places it above the 50 midpoint. However, the core corpus-query functions of BlackLabQueryExecutor (executeCqlQuery, executeDependencyPattern, executeSurfacePattern) have no stub-based or static-path coverage at all, and the indexing pipeline is explicitly disabled with no workaround — meaning the system's most critical runtime path has zero automatic verification. This structural gap caps the score well below 80.

strengths: Uniform error contract applied via wrapWithErrorHandling across all 11 endpoints — a developer can predict how failures propagate without reading each handler
CollocateQueryPort / QueryExecutor interface split is applied correctly: handlers that only need BCQL retrieval declare the narrower type, making dependency surfaces explicit
Consistent query-parameter utilities (requireParam, parseIntParam, parseDoubleParam) used uniformly — no hand-rolled parsing scattered across handlers
ExplorationService interface is clean: 4 focused methods, checked IOException, @NonNull annotations, clear Javadoc distinguishing single-seed vs multi-seed vs comparison modes
api_surface_coherence.issue_character: The issues are localized pattern-consistency problems within the api and model packages rather than structural API contract violations — the external HTTP surface is coherent, but internal response-assembly and model-construction conventions drift across sibling types.
api_surface_coherence.score_rationale: The codebase sits at the solid-but-uneven anchor (80): the external HTTP surface is disciplined and a developer can predict parameter names, error codes, and response envelope shape across most endpoints. What pulls the score below 80 is a recurring pattern drift that a developer extending the API will encounter: typed ExamplesResponse records coexist with raw Map<String,Object> response assembly for every other endpoint, and ComparisonResult's protected constructor conflicts with ExplorationResult's private-constructor-plus-factory discipline just two files away. These inconsistencies are module-level friction rather than contract violations, but they require a developer to make a judgment call every time they add a new endpoint or model type.

strengths: Security model is explicitly documented in WordSketchApiServer Javadoc and the loopback-only bind is enforced in code (InetAddress.getLoopbackAddress()), not just in documentation.
Every endpoint is registered through the same handler pipeline with identical error-handling wrappers — there are no siblings where one has a guard and another does not.

The highest-risk endpoint (/api/bcql, arbitrary corpus queries) has proportionally stronger controls: length limits, complexity limits, and an audit-intent log line, which is appropriate escalation rather than inconsistency.
CORS policy is applied uniformly because setCorsHeader is embedded in every response helper, making it structurally impossible to omit it on any response path.
Input validation utilities (requireParam, parseIntParam, parseDoubleParam, readBodyWithSizeLimit) are centralised and reused consistently across all handlers.
authorization_consistency.issue_character: The codebase has no authorization inconsistency in the conventional sense; the single structural concern is that the entire security model is a single-point-of-failure network assumption — if the bind address changes, there is no backup auth layer, and the BCQL audit log relies on a mutable log level rather than an append-only audit trail.
authorization_consistency.score_rationale: This is a local research tool with a deliberately documented 'loopback-only, no auth' policy applied with full consistency: every endpoint, every response path, every error path is treated identically. By the rubric's criteria — siblings with auth on some and not others, magic permission literals, mixed trust boundaries — there are essentially no defects. The score is modestly below exemplary because the security model has a structural single point of failure (bind address is not enforced beyond start()), the BCQL audit log is a best-effort INFO log rather than a structured audit trail, and there is no defence-in-depth option (API key, basic auth) documented for deployment scenarios where loopback binding is not feasible.

issues.summary: ExplorationService declares throws IOException but implementation throws unchecked ExplorationException, causing exploration failures to be misclassified in error boundary
issues.suggestion: Align the interface and implementation exception contract: change ExplorationService to declare 'throws ExplorationException' on all four methods (removing 'throws IOException'), and update ExplorationHandlers method signatures accordingly. Then add 'catch (ExplorationException e)' to wrapWithErrorHandling before the generic Exception catch, mapping it to a 500 with the message 'description + failed: ' + e.getMessage() (matching the IOException path's log level and message format). This gives exploration failures predictable log entries and makes the interface contract accurate.
evidence: ExplorationService.java lines 39, 47, 54, 63: all four interface methods declare 'throws IOException'.
SemanticFieldExplorer.java lines 97, 141, 160, 198: all four implementation methods declare 'throws ExplorationException' (which extends RuntimeException) — never IOException.
ExplorationException wraps IOException in its body (lines 111-112, 144-145, 168-169, 206-207) but the unchecked wrapper is never declared on the interface.
HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling catches IOException and Exception separately; ExplorationException falls into 'catch (Exception e)' logged as 'unexpected error', masking expected corpus failures.

ExplorationHandlers handler methods declare 'throws IOException' due to the interface, but IOException can never propagate — it is always absorbed into ExplorationException before reaching the handler.

issues.summary: ExplorationService declares throws IOException but implementation throws unchecked ExplorationException
issues.suggestion: Remove 'throws IOException' from all four ExplorationService interface methods to match the actual unchecked-exception contract. ExplorationException is already a RuntimeException and propagates without needing to be declared. Update ExploreHandlers handler method signatures accordingly (they currently declare 'throws IOException' to satisfy the interface). This makes the actual contract visible: callers must catch ExplorationException (or handle it via a general RuntimeException handler) rather than catching IOException.
evidence: ExplorationService.java declares all four methods with 'throws IOException': exploreByRelation, exploreMultiSeed, compareCollocateProfiles, fetchExamples.
SemanticFieldExplorer overrides those methods declaring 'throws ExplorationException'. ExplorationException extends RuntimeException, not IOException.
ExplorationException Javadoc states 'the ExplorationService interface stays free of checked exceptions', directly contradicting the interface's 'throws IOException' declarations.

ExplorationHandlers catches nothing from the service calls — it declares 'throws IOException' in each handler and relies on wrapWithErrorHandling's catch(Exception e) to intercept ExplorationExceptions. A caller who properly handles IOException (as the interface contract promises) will not catch ExplorationException.
The 'throws IOException' on the interface methods is dead — they can be removed without compile error because ExplorationException is unchecked.
